1:
The witnesses read the chariots, the horses, and the fifty runners as the first visible symptom of an ambition long nursed in secret. The grammatical-historical commentators set the political scene. Keil and Delitzsch treat the whole display as the opening move of a calculated campaign: restored to favour, Absalom soon began to aspire to the throne, setting up a princely court and endeavouring to turn the hearts of the people toward himself. Charles Ellicott supplies the logic — to mount a rebellion Absalom must first impress the people with his wealth and splendour — and notes the exact parallel of Adonijah, who likewise prepared chariots and horsemen and fifty men to run before him (1 Kings 1:5); this, he adds, was the first use in Israel of chariots and horses as a piece of regal pomp.

The Wesleyan voices dwell on the man and his motive. John Wesley observes that as the king's eldest surviving son — Amnon now dead and Chileab either dead or incapable — Absalom knew this parade would draw the eyes of the people to him and make them conclude that David intended him for his successor (compare the order of David's sons in 2 Sam. 3:2-5). Adam Clarke gathers from Calmet a whole portrait: a bold, violent, revengeful, haughty, enterprising, magnificent, eloquent, and popular prince, rich and vain of his personal accomplishments, royal in blood on both sides through his mother Maacah, daughter of Talmai king of Geshur. His one crime, Clarke insists, was that he sought the crown during his father's life, endeavouring to dethrone David in order to reign in his stead.

The Puritan Matthew Henry turns the pageantry into a lesson on the two things that recommend a man to popular esteem — greatness and goodness — both of which Absalom will counterfeit. He looks great: having learned at Geshur what was forbidden the kings of Israel, he multiplies horses, so that he looked desirable while his father, on his mule, looked despicable. Henry hears Samuel's old warning fulfilled — the king shall have chariots and horsemen, and some shall run before his chariots (1 Sam. 8:11) — and reads David's connivance as the fatal indulgence of a fond parent: those parents know not what they do who indulge a proud humour in their children, for more young people are ruined by pride than by any one lust whatsoever.

The Reformed Gill fixes the moment precisely — after this, that is, after the reconciliation of David and Absalom and his readmission to court — so that the very favour lately shown becomes the platform for treason. There is no real division among the witnesses here, only a converging judgement: the running footmen are not innocent splendour but the studied theatre of a usurper. Henry's word stands over the whole scene as its warning — those are commonly most eager for the honour of rule who least understand its duties, and a father's tender credulity can be the very soil in which a son's rebellion takes root.
2:
The witnesses read this verse as the demagogue taking up his station, and they agree that every detail is stagecraft. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the setting: Absalom stood beside the way of the gate, the very place where causes were heard and judgment given, and there intercepted each man who came to the king with a controversy, asking of what city he was. The gate was Israel's seat of justice, so to plant himself there was to plant himself in the office he coveted; K&D lingers over the Hebrew of the wish soon to follow, the emphatic 'who might appoint me judge,' by which Absalom throws himself forward as the people's advocate before he has any commission to be so.

The Wesleyan Wesley catches the theatre of it in two strokes. The early rising is a shew of solicitude for the good of the public and of every private person; the calling out to each suitor is a studied preventing him with the offers of his assistance, as one eager to make particular enquiry into the state of his cause. The diligence is real labour but hollow in aim — a performance mounted entirely for effect.

The Reformed Gill reads the same rising as calculated industry: Absalom rose up early every morning to show how diligent and industrious he would be were he in any office under the king, and especially when he should himself be king — the whole aimed to ingratiate himself into the affections of the people. Here the witnesses do not contend but converge on a single irony. What wears the look of a prince's conscientious care for justice is in truth the counterfeiting of the one virtue whose absence he will next charge upon his father: accessible, painstaking government. He questions each man's city because he means to canvass every tribe, informing himself of the whole state of the kingdom under the guise of sympathy. That a man once condemned in his own conscience for the murder of Amnon should pose as the friend of every wronged suitor is the deep hypocrisy the commentators feel beneath the courtesy; the posture of a servant has become the instrument of usurpation, and the very gate of judgment the place where judgment is undermined.
3:
Here Absalom passes from courtesy to insinuation, and the witnesses fasten on the two-edged craft of his words: he flatters the suitor and slanders the government in a single breath. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott exposes the technique with precision. There really were, he explains, officials appointed to hear causes and report the facts to the king, who then pronounced judgment, for it was impossible for the king to hear every case in detail; Absalom does not accuse David himself of wrong but insinuates that the whole system of government is defective, and then parades his own earnest wish to set things right. This, Ellicott observes, is precisely the art that the demagogue has used in every age.

The Wesleyan Wesley reads the same verse as a double stroke. Upon the slightest hearing of a cause — one side only — Absalom pronounces it good, that he might oblige all; and by there is no man deputed of the king to hear thee he means that none such will do the suitor justice, the other sons and rulers being wholly corrupted or at least careless, while the aged king is grown negligent of public affairs. Wesley draws the abiding moral: it is the way of turbulent, aspiring men to reproach the government they are under, and even David, the best of kings, could not escape the worst of censures.

The Reformed Gill notes the haste of the verdict — after some further talk, and finding the man had a suit to bring, Absalom at once declared the cause right without hearing the other party. The witnesses converge on the injustice folded inside the promise of justice: a man who will judge on one side's telling is no fit judge at all, however loud his complaint that no one is deputed to judge. The flattery costs Absalom nothing and buys him everything, for a suitor sent away with his cause pronounced good will believe there is no justice to be had except through Absalom, and will make all the interest he can for the prince who alone seemed to care. The commentators see in it the settled method by which discontent is manufactured: first persuade men they are wronged, then present yourself as their only remedy.
4:
This is the shortest notice in the chapter, and the source before the witnesses is correspondingly thin — the Reformed Gill alone glosses it, and only to say that Absalom spoke to the same persons, at the same time, pressing his campaign without pause. Yet the verse itself carries the naked wish that the surrounding commentary has been circling, and it repays a closer look at its own words: Oh that I were made judge in the land, that every man which hath any suit or cause might come unto me, and I would do him justice!

Gill's brevity fixes the point that matters: this is not a stray complaint but a link in a chain, uttered deliberately to the very men whose causes he had just pronounced good. Having first persuaded each suitor that no one is appointed to hear him (v.3), Absalom now supplies the remedy the manufactured grievance requires — himself. The wish is artfully framed. He does not petition his father for a judgeship, nor offer to serve under the existing rulers; he asks only that men might come unto him, that in all causes and over all persons he might preside. It is the language of service — I would do him justice — laid over the substance of supremacy.

The witnesses elsewhere in the chapter name the irony that hangs over this verse, and it belongs here: a man who should himself have stood trial for the murder of his brother Amnon now covets the seat from which others are judged, and one who has just shown he will hear a cause on one side only advertises himself as the fountain of impartial justice. There is no debate to record; the single witness and the verse's own words agree that Absalom's O that I were a judge is the hinge of his whole design, the point at which flattery of the people turns into a bid for their allegiance. Those most ambitious of a place, the older expositors add, are commonly the least fit to fill it, and the modesty of the truly qualified is the opposite of the spirit that cries, without commission or conscience, that it alone could set the nation right.
5:
The witnesses read the outstretched hand and the kiss as the last and most winning touch of Absalom's art, the affability that seals the whole performance. The Reformed Gill sets the scene simply: when any man came near to do him obeisance — to pay his respects and bow to him as the king's son, a prince of the blood, and the supposed heir to the crown — Absalom would not let him stoop, but put forth his hand, took hold of him, and kissed him. The gesture refuses the distance that rank allows and offers instead the embrace of a friend.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the same act and reach for a classical parallel: it was by conduct exactly of this kind, they note, that Agamemnon is said to have secured the command of the Grecian army. The condescension is not accidental but the studied instrument of a man building a following; the prince who will not be bowed to makes himself the more irresistibly the object of the people's love. K&D read the verse as the immediate cause of the summary that follows — this manner, repeated to all Israel, is how Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel (v.6).

The witnesses do not divide; they observe together the deep duplicity of the caress. To take a subject by the hand and kiss him is, in the manner of the East, the warmest token of equality and regard, and Absalom spends it lavishly upon men he means only to use. The older expositors elsewhere name the heart of it — his conduct could not be more condescending while his heart was as proud as Lucifer's, and ambitious projects are often carried on under a show of humility. Here is flattery perfected: not the flattery of words only, which the previous verses supplied, but of the body, the personal touch that persuades each common man he has a friend at court who counts him an equal. The kiss that Absalom offers is the kiss of the betrayer, courtesy turned into a snare, and the witnesses feel beneath its warmth the cold calculation of a man drawing the people into his net.
6:
This verse gathers the previous scenes into a verdict — so Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel — and the witnesses concentrate on the force of the word stole. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch do the lexical work. The Hebrew phrase, they explain, does not mean to deceive or cheat, as the same verb does in another conjugation at Genesis 31:20, but literally to steal the heart — to bring a person over to one's side secretly and by stratagem. The theft is of affection, quietly diverted from the rightful king to the pretender before anyone perceives the loss.

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the political character out of the summary. His manner of doing this, he says, has been circumstantially related in the preceding verses: Absalom was thoroughly versed in the arts of the demagogue, and the common people heard him gladly. He used the patriot's arguments and was everything of the kind, so far as promise could go; he found fault with the men in power, and only wanted their place — like all other pretended patriots — that he might act as they did, or worse. Clarke's sting is that the reform Absalom promised was never the aim, only the ladder.

The Reformed Gill knits the verse to the method: to all Israel that came for judgment Absalom told them there was no justice to be had, wished himself in office to give it, and behaved in the loving manner just described — and by this threefold craft of slander, promise, and caress he won them. The witnesses converge without dispute on the pathos beneath the politics. A whole people is being carried away, not by force or by any genuine grievance proved, but by a stolen affection; and the theft is the more grievous because its victim is David, a king who had not, the commentators note, done anything to forfeit the love of his people. Gill adds the sober measure of the loss — the men of Israel here are the generality, the leading men whose defection would carry the crowd with them — so that what Absalom pilfered was not a handful of malcontents but the loyalty of a nation. The heart, once stolen, will follow where it is led; the older expositors elsewhere warn that the majority is no certain rule to judge equity by, for all the world may wonder after a beast. The next verses show the quiet theft ripening into open revolt.
7:
With the conspiracy ripe, Absalom asks leave to pay a vow at Hebron, and the witnesses divide sharply over one word before uniting on the pretext behind it. The great crux is the number forty years. The grammatical-historical commentators judge the text corrupt: Keil and Delitzsch call the figure altogether unsuitable, since it can relate neither to Absalom's age nor to David's reign — Absalom was born after David began to reign, and David reigned but forty years and a half in all. Charles Ellicott agrees the reading is certainly incorrect and, with the Syriac, most manuscripts of the Vulgate, and Josephus, prefers four years, probably reckoned from Absalom's return to Jerusalem. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke marshals the same versional evidence — the Syriac and Arabic read four, Theodoret reads four — while noting two of Kennicott's manuscripts read forty days; yet he grants that four years, dated perhaps from the slaying of Amnon, best fits the story.

Against emendation stand the harmonizers. John Wesley and, as one option, the Puritan Matthew Henry keep the forty years by dating them not from David's reign but from the change of the government into a monarchy — the people's first demand for a king — which Henry places about ten years before David began to reign, so that the rebellion falls about the thirtieth year of his reign. The Reformed Gill canvasses every proposal — forty years from Israel's first king, from Saul's slaughter of the priests at Nob, from David's anointing by Samuel — and, finding none satisfactory, concludes that one may be tempted to suspect a copyist's error of arbaim for arba, forty for four, which makes all easy.

On the vow itself the witnesses close ranks. Henry frankly doubts Absalom ever made such a vow — it does not appear he was so religiously inclined — but a man who stuck at neither murder nor treason would not make conscience of a lie; and none could disprove him. Ellicott is gentler, allowing that a vow made during exile is not improbable, though Absalom now uses it purely as a pretext, veiling his crime under the cloak of religion and receiving his father's blessing at the very moment he strikes at his crown and life. Hebron they agree was well chosen: the place of Absalom's birth, an ancient holy city, David's own first capital for seven years, and possibly full of men who resented the removal of the throne to Jerusalem. The debate over the date is real and unresolved; the judgement on the piety is unanimous — this is religion made a stalking-horse to sedition.
8:
Absalom fills out his petition with the words of the vow itself, and the witnesses weigh both its geography and its hollow piety. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply record the terms — while he abode at Geshur in Syria he vowed that, if the LORD would indeed bring him again to Jerusalem, then he would serve the LORD — and read them as the plausible cover story that gains him leave to depart for Hebron, from which the rebellion will spring.

The Wesleyan Clarke raises a sharp topographical objection. Geshur, the country of Talmai, was not in Syria at all but lay to the south of Canaan, near Edom, as is plain from the campaigns and movements recorded in Judg. 1:10, 1 Sam. 27:8, and 2 Sam. 13:37; he concludes that Aram (Syria) is likely a scribal slip for Edom, the two words differing by a single easily confused letter, and notes that Edom is in fact the reading of the Syriac and Arabic. On the vow's substance Clarke is scathing: here Absalom pretended to be a strict follower of Jehovah even in a heathen country, and now craves liberty to perform at Hebron a vow he claims to have made there — all the more perfectly to organize his system of rebellion against his venerable father.

The Reformed Gill adds only the plain note that the vow was made at his grandfather's court in Geshur, leaving the irony to speak for itself. The witnesses do not divide over meaning; they converge on the audacity of the profession. The clause I will serve the LORD is the language of devotion pressed into the service of betrayal, and the very solemnity of a vow — the most binding of religious acts — is what makes the deceit effective, for a father could hardly refuse a son who spoke of paying his debts to God. The commentators let the contrast stand as the measure of Absalom's hypocrisy: the man who names the LORD as the object of his service is at that instant aiming at his father's throne and life, and the piety that should have humbled him is the very mask he wears to strike.
9:
David grants the request in two words — Go in peace — and the witnesses read the brief exchange as the tragic pivot where a father's love is turned into the door of his own ruin. The Reformed Gill notes simply that the king gave him leave to go, wishing that happiness and prosperity might attend him. The blessing is sincere; the son who receives it is a traitor.

The Wesleyan Wesley turns to the place Absalom named, drawing out why Hebron was so shrewd a choice. It was an eminent city, next to Jerusalem the chief of the tribe of Judah, and the place where David himself had begun his kingdom — which Absalom took for a good omen — and where it is probable he had already secured many friends; it lay, too, at a convenient distance from Jerusalem, near enough to strike and far enough to gather strength unobserved. Wesley's reading exposes the calculation folded inside the pious errand: the vow chose the one city best fitted to be the cradle of a revolt.

The witnesses do not divide, and the source here is brief, so the verse's own irony must carry the weight the commentators feel. Go in peace is the customary Hebrew farewell, a benediction of shalom, and David speaks it in the fullness of a reconciled affection, glad that his once-estranged son now professes to seek the LORD. Yet the peace he pronounces is the very thing Absalom goes to destroy, and the leave he grants is the last free gift of a king who will next be on foot in the wilderness. The older expositors elsewhere name the lesson that hangs over this moment — how willing tender parents are to believe the best concerning their children, and how easily that credulity is abused by a child who wears the show of religion. David's generosity is not his fault but his glory; the guilt is wholly Absalom's, who takes a father's blessing as the cover for a son's treason, and rises from the audience to go straight to Hebron and raise the standard of rebellion.
10:
Absalom's spies fan out with a single signal, and the witnesses examine both the machinery of the plot and the secrecy that has cloaked it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the word spies: the messengers are so called because they were first to ascertain the feelings of the people tribe by tribe, and only to execute their commission — to raise the cry — where they could reckon on support. K&D stress how close the conspiracy had been kept, pointing forward to the two hundred guests who went in their simplicity, knowing nothing at all of the affair (v.11). The arrangement is that of a coordinated rising: at the sound of the trumpet, every planted agent is to proclaim Absalom reigneth in Hebron.

Charles Ellicott fills in the method. The agents were to sound the people in the various parts of the land and to communicate the conspiracy only secretly, and only to those found favourably disposed; they may have left Jerusalem with Absalom, or been sent out quietly, a few at a time, beforehand. The Wesleyan Clarke seizes on the trumpet as the ingenious instrument of the whole: the signal was to pass from place to place, trumpet exciting trumpet across the country, so that in a few minutes all Israel might hear the proclamation — a kind of telegraph by which a scattered plot could ignite as one.

The Reformed Gill reads the sending in the same key: the spies went throughout all the land to sound the disposition of the people, to insinuate things into their minds in Absalom's favour, and to improve every opportunity of recommending him to their esteem and affection. Here the witnesses do not contend; they trace together the anatomy of a well-laid rebellion — the careful prior canvassing of hearts, the secrecy that spared no man a premature alarm, the single audible signal that would turn private disaffection into a public acclamation. What Absalom had stolen quietly, verse by verse and kiss by kiss, is now to be cashed in all at once. The commentators mark the terrible efficiency of it, and the reader feels the ground shift under David even before the messenger reaches him: the hearts were already gone, and only the trumpet was wanting.
11:
Two hundred men of Jerusalem go down to Hebron in perfect innocence, and the witnesses read them as the most poignant instrument of Absalom's craft — good men made the unwitting scenery of a revolt. The grammatical-historical commentators stress their standing and their ignorance. Charles Ellicott takes them for prominent and influential citizens, invited to share in the sacrifices, whose entire ignorance of Absalom's purpose shows the extreme secrecy with which the affair was managed; once at Hebron, Absalom hoped either to win them to his side or, failing that, forcibly to prevent their opposition, and in any case their presence would make the people suppose the great men of the city were with him. Keil and Delitzsch underline the text's own emphasis — they went in their simplicity and knew nothing at all of the affair — reading it as proof of how tightly the plot had been sealed.

The Wesleyan voices sharpen the point of the deception. Adam Clarke takes the two hundred for soldiers whom Absalom judged would be of consequence to him, seduced by his specious conduct yet knowing nothing of his design. John Wesley presses the political calculation: Absalom picked out men of reputation with king and people to lend his undertaking countenance, so that observers might at first suppose the whole was done by David's own consent, the aged king willing to resign the throne to his son. Wesley draws the abiding lesson — it is no new thing for good men to be made use of by designing men to put a colour upon ill practices.

The Reformed Gill reports the same innocence, adding the rabbinic tradition that Absalom obtained leave to invite only two, then asked two more unknown to the first, and so on, two by two, until they numbered two hundred — a small parable of how a conspiracy grows by imperceptible steps. The witnesses do not divide; they converge on the pathos of it. These men are not traitors but the furniture of treason, their reputations borrowed without their knowledge to prop a cause they would have abhorred. When religion is made a stalking-horse and a sacrifice a pretext, the older expositors observe, even the well-affected may be drawn to lend their names to what in their hearts they detest, not having known the depths of the design that used them.
12:
The summoning of Ahithophel is, for the witnesses, the most dangerous stroke of the whole conspiracy — the moment a reckless plot gains a master's brain. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the scene: Giloh, Ahithophel's town, lay just south of Hebron, so he may have gone there in readiness to be summoned. Why he deserted David, Ellicott admits, does not appear. He canvasses the old conjecture that Ahithophel was aggrieved over David's treatment of Bathsheba, supposed to be his granddaughter — her father Eliam and Ahithophel's son Eliam being possibly one — but doubts it, since a grandfather would more likely be honoured than aggrieved that his daughter was made queen. He judges it likelier that Ahithophel and many of Judah were alienated as their relative importance shrank in David's expanding empire, noting that the rebellion was cradled in Judah and found its chief strength there.

Ellicott presses the parallel that has always haunted this figure. Ahithophel's conduct, both in his treachery and his suicide, forms a striking likeness to Judas, to whom the same words of the trusted friend who ate David's bread are applied (John 13:18); many writers, he adds, take Ps. 41:9 and Ps. 55 to have been composed with reference to him. The Wesleyan Clarke states the gravity plainly — in gaining Ahithophel, Absalom in effect got the prime minister of the kingdom to join him — while Wesley exposes the sacrifices as mere machinery: Absalom offered them not in devotion to God but that upon this pretence he might call great numbers of people together.

The Reformed Gill relays the tradition that Ahithophel was grandfather to Bathsheba and so resented David's adultery and the murder of Uriah, and adds Absalom's motive in sending for him — to get out of him David's secrets, he being reckoned the best counsellor in the land. Ellicott's note that the sacrifices ran on with pomp for several days shows how the feasting itself served the plot, giving Absalom room to work his popular arts until, as the verse closes, the conspiracy grew strong and the people increased continually with him. The witnesses converge on the ominous arithmetic: a discontented statesman, a growing crowd, and a religious festival for cover — the revolt now has both a head and a body.
13:
The messenger's word — the hearts of the men of Israel are after Absalom — lands on David, and the witnesses read both the political blow and the providence behind it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the idiom: to have the heart after a man is to be attached to him as king, and David hears in it the threat of the very calamity now overtaking him. K&D read his instant resolve to flee as fear that Jerusalem might be betrayed into Absalom's hands, and as the judgment foretold when Nathan warned that evil would rise against him out of his own house (2 Sam. 12:10-11).

The Puritan Matthew Henry weighs the report and the man. The matter was bad enough, he says, and, as such tidings commonly are, made worse in the telling — that the generality, or at least the leading men, were after Absalom — yet David the more readily believed it because he could now call to mind the arts by which Absalom had inveigled them, and reproached himself for not having done more to secure his own interest, of which he had been too confident. Henry draws the maxim: it is the wisdom of princes to make sure of their subjects, for he who has their hearts has their hands and all at his service.

The Wesleyan Clarke confesses the general defection is hard to account for and lists the causes that may have combined: David grown old and less able to administer justice; the lack of properly appointed judges; the overgrown and dreaded power of Joab; the lingering partisans of Saul's house; David's own standing under the divine displeasure for the matter of Bathsheba and Uriah; the restless and disaffected who hope for gain from any change; and the natural instinct of most to hail the rising rather than the setting sun, Absalom being the apparent heir. The Reformed Gill supposes the messenger was one of the two hundred, who, discovering the design, disapproved it and hastened to warn the king. The witnesses converge: the alarm is real and the danger great, but beneath the politics they hear the word of the LORD beginning to be fulfilled, and David bowing to a rod he knows he has deserved.
14:
David's sudden decision to abandon Jerusalem draws from the witnesses two readings of a single act — humble submission and shrewd generalship — held together in one man. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the military sense. Charles Ellicott judges the sequel proved the wisdom of it: Ahithophel and Hushai alike recognized that delay would be fatal to Absalom, whose unreasonable rebellion must lose ground with time given for reflection; by withdrawing, David also spared the city much of the horror of civil war and saved Jerusalem from the edge of the sword. Keil and Delitzsch read the same urgency — David flees at once lest Absalom overtake him suddenly, smite the city, and let his adherents be scattered before they can gather.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke, however, is struck that David should flee at all. This, he observes, was the first time David turned his back to an enemy; Jerusalem was so strong that even the blind and the lame were reckoned defence enough for its walls (2 Sam. 5:6), and he still had his faithful Cherethites, Pelethites, and six hundred Gittites. Clarke's answer is that David took all as from the hand of God: he humbles himself, weeps, goes barefoot, covers his head, and does not even hasten as a man flying from a foe, for the habit of a mourner is not the habit of the hunted — he sees the storm and yields to what he takes for the tempest of the Almighty. John Wesley supplies the practical calculation that stands beside the piety: though the fort of Zion was strong, David had laid in no provisions for a siege, and if once shut up there Absalom would seize the whole kingdom; marching abroad, he might raise a considerable army for his defence.

So the great question the witnesses circle is why flee — and they answer at two levels without contradicting one another. The Puritan Matthew Henry frames it best, reading the flight either as the act of a penitent submitting to the rod, who before unrighteous Absalom could justify himself but before the righteous God must condemn himself and yield, or as the act of a politician who would not make a great and half-fortified city the seat of war. The Reformed Gill simply notes David addressing his resolve to the servants and officers still with him in the city. The verse holds both truths at once: David is at the same moment the wise commander choosing open country over a trap and the broken king accepting the punishment of his iniquity.
15:
Against the dark background of a nation's defection, this verse sets a single bright note — the unshaken loyalty of David's own servants — and the witnesses, though the source here is brief, make it carry weight. The Reformed Gill gives the plain sense: the king's servants answered him to show that they were entirely conformable to his pleasure, ready, in the verse's own words, to do whatsoever their lord the king should appoint. Their reply is unconditional; they do not weigh the danger, ask the destination, or bargain for terms, but place themselves wholly at his disposal.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the servants' declaration as the hinge of the departure — because they professed themselves ready to follow, the king went out of the city with all his household in his train. The pledge is not idle words; it immediately becomes action, the whole company moving at his feet into exile. The Puritan Matthew Henry, tracing the same moment, notes that David's servants faithfully adhered to him and assured him of their inviolable allegiance, and that the method by which Absalom had gained the fickle multitude made no impression upon them; they knew David's affection to them and his office, and steadily held their ground.

The witnesses do not divide, and the very brevity of the verse throws its contrast into relief. The men of Israel had let their hearts be stolen by a flatterer's kiss and a false promise of justice; these servants, offered no bribe and facing only hardship, bind themselves the tighter to a king in his humiliation. It is a picture of love proved by adversity — the friend who loves at all times and cleaves to us when fortune has fled. The older expositors elsewhere draw the pattern to its fulfilment: so should we cleave to the Son of David with full purpose of heart, following him not for wealth or greatness — for then we would desert him in his low estate — but for his own worth, that neither life nor death may separate us from his love. Here, in the mouth of a few faithful courtiers, is the loyalty a whole kingdom had forgotten.
16:
The king goes forth with his whole household, leaving ten concubines to keep the house, and the witnesses read the departure at once as an act of humility and as the quiet turning of a prophecy. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the movement: the king went out with all his family at his feet, halting once outside the city at what they take to be a proper name — the farthest house from the city, on the road toward Jericho — where the fugitive company gathered before pressing on.

The Wesleyan Wesley draws out the manner and the motive of the going. David went forth on foot, choosing to humble himself under the hand of God, to encourage his companions in this hard and comfortless march, and to move his people to compassion. Of the ten women left behind, Wesley says David supposed their sex would protect them and their relation to him gain them some respect, or at least safety, from his son — a reasonable expectation of ordinary decency. The Reformed Gill marks only that the king's going forth determined the case: the resolve to flee, once he set out, was past recall.

Here the witnesses feel a solemn undertow beneath David's prudence. The concubines left to keep the house are, in the older expositors' reading, the unwitting occasion by which God overruled David's own arrangement to fulfil his word — for the very wives he thought their modesty would shield were those over whom Nathan had said his neighbour would lie in the sight of the sun, evil raised against him out of his own house (compare 2 Sam. 12:11). David acts from natural tenderness and common expectation, yet a purpose deeper than his own is working through his flight. The scene is thus double: on its surface a father shepherding his family into the wilderness, and beneath it the steady outworking of a sentence the king had brought upon himself, so that even his precautions become the means by which the prophecy against him is accomplished. The witnesses do not contend; they let the two levels stand together, the man doing what seemed wise while God did what he had spoken.
17:
The king halts at a place on the edge of the city, and the witnesses read the pause as a muster — the moment a scattered flight is marshalled into an ordered march. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses for precision against the vaguer English: better than a place that was far off is the far house, a definite spot known by that name, probably the last house on the outskirts of the city before the road crossed the Kidron; here, he says, David mustered his forces and made the arrangements for his flight. The stop is not indecision but organization.

The Wesleyan voices fill in the human detail. Adam Clarke supposes David waited there until he saw all his friends safely out of the city, unwilling to press forward while any faithful follower might still be within the walls. John Wesley reads the distance modestly — a convenient stopping-place, though not very far off — enough to be clear of the city yet near enough to gather those still coming. The Reformed Gill hears in the repeated phrase the king went forth the note of haste and agitation, the narrator dwelling on the going out to convey the hurry and fright in which it was done.

The witnesses do not divide, and the picture they compose is of a king who, even in the confusion of exile, does not simply run. He stops, counts his people, sets them in rank, and waits for the last of his friends — the conduct of a shepherd and a soldier at once. The commentators feel the pathos of it against the earlier pageantry: the man who a chapter ago sat in his palace now stands at the farthest house of his own capital, taking the muster of the faithful few who will share his wandering. The pause at the far house becomes, in their reading, a small emblem of David's whole bearing in the flight — deliberate, unhurried by panic, careful of others before himself, and composed enough under God's hand to order his going even as his kingdom slips behind him.
18:
As the loyal forces file past their king, the witnesses fasten on one puzzling name — the Gittites — and divide over who these six hundred men from Gath really were. The grammatical-historical commentators identify them with David's oldest companions. Keil and Delitzsch insist they cannot be actual Philistines, for the apposition explains them as the six hundred who came in his train from Gath — the very band that gathered round David in his flight from Saul. Charles Ellicott traces their whole history under that name: though the word would naturally mean men of Gath, it is here defined as the six hundred who came after him from Gath, the company familiar from David's outlawry at the cave (1 Sam. 22:1-2), at Keilah (1 Sam. 23:13), in the wilderness, and at Ziklag, who went up with him to Hebron and became in time his corps of mighty men.

Against this the Wesleyan Wesley takes the Gittites for genuine foreigners — rather strangers, he says, Philistines by birth, born in the city or territory of Gath, whom David's counsel and example had won to embrace the true religion, and who had proved their military skill, valour, and fidelity. The difference is real: were these six hundred Israelite veterans nicknamed from their long sojourn among the Philistines, or a body of Gentile proselytes lately attached to David's service? The Reformed Gill leaves the question open, noting only that the servants passed on at the king's side while the guards went before him.

Yet beneath the dispute the witnesses agree on what the scene displays: the astonishing constancy of David's inner circle. K&D and Ellicott stress that whoever these men were, they were seasoned soldiers bound to David through every reversal of his fortunes, now closing around him in his deepest humiliation as they had in his first exile. The bodyguard of Cherethites and Pelethites and this hardened six hundred pass on before the king in good order, an army in miniature marching out of a lost capital. The commentators let the picture make its own point: the crown may be stolen and the nation seduced, but the men who had shared David's wilderness years do not waver, and their faithfulness — foreign or homebred — shames the fickle multitude that Absalom had bought with a kiss.
19:
David turns to Ittai the Gittite and urges him to go back, and the witnesses read the exchange as a study in the king's generosity — he will not drag a newcomer into his ruin. The grammatical-historical commentators establish who Ittai was. Keil and Delitzsch take him for a Philistine commander who had come over to David not long before, later trusted with a third of the army beside Joab and Abishai (2 Sam. 18:2), and who had brought his whole family with him. Charles Ellicott agrees the patronymic is literal, since David calls him a stranger and an exile, and reads the king's counsel plainly: return and abide with the king means that Ittai, as a foreigner, need not entangle himself in Israel's quarrel but is free to serve whatever king holds Jerusalem; David neither recognizes Absalom nor speaks ironically, but releases Ittai from a cause not his own.

The Wesleyan voices read the king more pointedly. John Wesley takes it of Absalom, now made king in fact, and glosses David's words as sending Ittai back to his settled home in Jerusalem, an exile not much concerned in David's affairs and so not fit to be involved in his troubles. Adam Clarke adds the tradition that Ittai may have been a son of Achish king of Gath, warmly attached to David and banished from his father's court for that attachment, he and his six hundred being generally reckoned proselytes to the Jewish religion. The Reformed Gill simply notes that Ittai commanded the band of six hundred Gittites.

The witnesses feel the nobility of David's tenderness even in his extremity. Ellicott catches it best by pairing Ittai's coming answer with Ruth's — whither thou goest I will go (Ruth 1:16-17) — for David's very effort to send him away is what draws out the stranger's undying loyalty. There is a real difference of nuance, whether the king Ittai is bidden to serve is Absalom named without irony (Wesley) or simply whatever ruler holds the city (Ellicott); but on the substance the commentators are one. A king with every reason to gather all the swords he can instead thinks first of the hardship a proselyte and a new convert would suffer, and generously offers to release him — and it is precisely this unselfishness that binds Ittai the faster.
20:
David presses his plea a step further — Ittai came but yesterday, why should he be made to wander today? — and dismisses him with a blessing, and the witnesses gather chiefly around the farewell words, mercy and truth be with thee. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the king's argument closely: thy coming is from yesterday, and should I disturb thee today to go with us, when I am going I know not whither? — David will not impose the uncertainty of his own homeless course on a man so newly arrived. K&D take the closing grace and truth to name the grace and faithfulness of God himself, which David invokes upon Ittai, comparing the pairing of mercy and truth in Prov. 3:3.

The Wesleyan voices dwell on the benediction as prayer. Adam Clarke hears in it a reciprocal wish — may God ever show thee mercy as thou showest it to me, and may his truth preserve thee from error and delusion. John Wesley expands it in the same key: unable now to recompense Ittai's kindness and fidelity himself, David prays that God would show him mercy, blessing him with every good, and faithfulness, making good all the promises he has made not to Israelites only but to all true-hearted proselytes such as Ittai is. The brethren Ittai is bidden to take back Wesley identifies as his countrymen, the fellow-Gittites of the previous verse.

The Reformed Gill notes simply that Ittai had come but yesterday from Gath or from some recent expedition, underscoring how fresh his attachment was. The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as the crown of David's unselfishness in the flight. Even now, hunted and uncertain of his own road, the king spends his thought on the welfare of a stranger, and, having nothing else to give, gives the richest thing he can — a prayer that the mercy and truth of God would go with the man he is trying to spare. It is the benediction of one who himself lives by that mercy and truth, casting his friend upon the same divine faithfulness on which he has staked his own comfort and hope, so that the poorest moment of David's reign yields one of the most generous words on his lips.
21:
Ittai answers David's plea with an oath of undying devotion, and the witnesses hear in it one of the noblest confessions of loyalty in Scripture. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the vow with care: Assuredly, in the place where my lord the king shall be, whether for death or for life, there also will thy servant be. K&D weigh the Hebrew particles that open the oath — a solemn surely — and defend the received text against a proposed emendation, so that the whole force of the asseveration stands: Ittai binds himself to David's fortunes without reserve, sharing his death if it comes to death and his life if it comes to life.

The Reformed Gill marks only that Ittai answered with an oath, but the very form matters: the stranger swears by the living LORD and by the life of the king, invoking the God of Israel as witness to a Gentile's fidelity. The oath is the counterpart of David's generosity in the preceding verses — the more the king urged him to depart in safety, the more absolutely Ittai refuses to be parted from him. Here is loyalty that is not bought and cannot be dismissed, cleaving to David not for his wealth or greatness, which have now fled, but for his own worth.

The Wesleyan Wesley lifts the verse at once to its highest use. He is a friend indeed, Wesley says, who loves at all times and will cleave to us in adversity; and thus should we cleave to the Son of David, that neither life nor death may separate us from his love. The witnesses do not divide; they read Ittai as the very picture of faith proved by trial. A whole nation had let its heart be stolen while its king prospered, and deserted him the moment his fortunes darkened; this foreigner, newly come and freely offered his release, swears to follow David into death itself. The older expositors have already found in such constancy a rebuke to fair-weather allegiance and a parable of the disciple's bond to Christ — the love that holds fast precisely where reward is gone and only hardship remains, and that therefore proves itself true.
22:
David yields to Ittai's constancy — Go and pass over — and the little company crosses the Kidron, and the witnesses read the brief verse for both its grammar and its scene. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the king's word: after so firm an assurance of devotion, David lets Ittai do as he pleases, and the phrase means not pass by but go forward — press on with us. K&D take the little ones who cross with him to stand, as often, for a man's whole family, so that Ittai brings household and all into David's exile, holding nothing back.

The Reformed Gill notes that, Ittai's resolve being fixed, David urged him no more but bade him pass over the brook Kidron before him — the courtesy of a king who lets his faithful friend go first. The crossing itself K&D describe: all the land wept aloud as the people went forward, and the king went over the Kidron, a winter torrent that runs only in the heavy rains, toward the way of the wilderness.

The witnesses do not divide, and the weight of the verse lies in what it quietly accomplishes. Ittai and his men and their families pass over with the king, and in passing over they seal the pledge of the previous verse in action — the oath becomes a boundary crossed, the Kidron put between them and the safety they might have kept in the city. The commentators feel the moment as the point of no return for these strangers: they have not merely promised to follow David but have now stepped, wives and children with them, out of Jerusalem and into his wandering. What David offered to spare them they have deliberately embraced, and the little band that files across the brook — servants, guards, and this foreign six hundred with their households — is the true remnant of the kingdom, small, hunted, and faithful, going forward into the wilderness at their king's side. Gill's small notice, that David let Ittai pass over before him, catches the courtesy that survives even in exile; and K&D's care over the plain sense of the verbs guards the scene from over-reading, keeping it what it is — a company on the march, moving forward, not lingering to look back at the city they have surrendered to a usurper.
23:
The whole countryside weeps as the king crosses the Kidron toward the wilderness, and the witnesses read the scene for its grief, its geography, and — most strikingly — its shadow of Christ. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the place. Charles Ellicott describes the Kidron as a valley with a watercourse, filled only in winter, lying immediately east of Jerusalem between the city and the Mount of Olives; Keil and Delitzsch add that it is a mountain torrent flowing only in the heavy rains, and both, with Adam Clarke, note the same detail — an inconsiderable brook, dry much of the year — pointing forward to the wilderness road that lay between Jerusalem and Jericho.

The Reformed Gill explains the weeping: the people of the country villages round about, hearing that the king had left Jerusalem because of his son's conspiracy, came out and wept aloud to see him driven into exile by a rebellious son. It is the sorrow of a land for a wronged king who had done nothing to forfeit their love — a public lamentation that measures, by contrast, the guilt of the multitude Absalom had seduced.

But the note the witnesses return to is typological, and here the schools speak with one voice. John Wesley marks that the Kidron is the very same brook that Christ passed over when he entered upon his sufferings (John 18:1), and the Puritan Matthew Henry presses the same likeness — the rejected king, betrayed by his own, going out over the Kidron in tears is a figure of great David's greater Son, who crossed that brook to Gethsemane. The commentators do not divide; they let the wilderness gather up the whole picture. David, the Lord's anointed, forsaken by his people and mourned by a faithful few, treads the sorrowful road that his Son would one day tread in earnest — so that the weeping land east of Jerusalem becomes, in the witnesses' reading, a rehearsal of a deeper passion, where the true King would be delivered up by a friend who had eaten his bread. The detail Gill and K&D preserve — that the whole land wept aloud as the people went forward — only deepens the likeness, for the same brook would one day be crossed in an even lonelier grief.
24:
Zadok and all the Levites come out bearing the ark of the covenant, and the witnesses read the scene as the great proof of the priesthood's fidelity to David. The Puritan Matthew Henry makes it the heart of the chapter's comfort: the priests and Levites knew David's affection to them and their office, and Absalom's arts had made no impression upon a man of so little religion; therefore they steadily adhered to David, ready to accompany him and carry the ark, that by it they might ask counsel of God for him. Henry draws the lesson — those who are friends to the ark in their prosperity will find it a friend to them in their adversity.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott clarifies the movement, which the Hebrew leaves obscure. Zadok appears here in charge of the ark; probably he and the Levites brought it out of the city and set it down while the multitude were assembling, meantime Abiathar leading the people forward up the Mount of Olives until all had come out of the city. The Wesleyan Clarke supplies the priests' reasoning: God had given the kingdom to David and had shown no sign of deposing him, so they chose to accompany him and take with them the ark, the sacred charge committed to their keeping.

The Reformed Gill simply names Zadok the priest at the head of the procession. The witnesses do not divide; they dwell together on the pathos and the loyalty of the moment. The ark of God itself is carried out of Jerusalem after the exiled king, as though the very symbol of the covenant would not stay behind with the usurper — and the priests who bear it, unmoved by the flattery that had turned a nation, cast their lot with a fugitive. Formerly, the older expositors recall, David would not rest until he had found a resting-place for the ark; now, if the priests may have their way, the ark shall not rest until David returns to his. The setting-down of the ark, while the people pass, becomes a silent tableau of a kingdom in suspense — the throne dispossessed, yet the worship of God still ranged on the side of the LORD's anointed.
25:
David orders the ark carried back into the city, and the witnesses read the command as the high point of his faith and self-denial in the flight. The Reformed Gill admits the reason is not easy to give — carried back, the ark would fall into the conspirators' hands, and the priests with it might have been of use to enquire of God — yet concludes that David judged so sacred a thing would not be violated by Absalom, and would be safest in the tabernacle he had built for it. The Wesleyan Clarke reads the same act as great confidence joined to great humility: the ark was too precious to be exposed to the hazards of his wandering, and David, unwilling to carry off from the city of God the very thing without which the public worship could not be maintained, felt more for that worship and the honour of God than for his own personal safety.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch find in the words the posture of a chastened man: David puts his whole fate, in believing confidence, into the hand of the LORD, because he feels it is the LORD who is chastising him through this rebellion. The condition David sets — if I shall find favour in the eyes of the LORD, he will bring me again, and show me both it and his habitation — makes his return depend not on his own arms but on God's mercy, the ark itself being the pledge he hopes one day to see again.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the devotion under the resolve. David is solicitous above all for the safety of the ark, more concerned for the church's prosperity than for his own, preferring Jerusalem before his chief joy (Ps. 137:6); and he longs to be brought back not chiefly to his palace and throne but to the enjoyment of God's house. John Wesley adds the tender motive of sparing the priests, whom David would not expose to Absalom's rage as he had once been forced to expose them to Saul's. The witnesses converge: the sending back of the ark is no lack of reverence but its very perfection — a king who will not clutch at the symbols of God's presence to secure himself, but commits both the ark and his own destiny to the God whose favour alone can restore him.
26:
David completes his surrender with words of perfect resignation — if he thus say, I have no delight in thee; behold, here am I, let him do to me as seemeth good unto him — and the witnesses read it as the summit of the chapter's penitence. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in it the voice of a man who knows he is under sentence: David recognizes that he suffers the punishment pronounced by Nathan for his sin, and seeks to throw himself entirely into the hands of God, trusting in his mercy. This, Ellicott adds, is why David will not carry the ark with him — lest he seem to compel the divine presence and blessing — sure that if God so will he shall be brought again in peace, and if not, resolved to submit himself wholly to God's ordering (compare his like word in 2 Sam. 24:14).

The Reformed Gill draws a fine distinction in the dreaded clause. If God should say he has no delight in David, Gill takes it of delight in him as a king and in his temporal prosperity, not of God's everlasting love toward him as a chosen vessel, a saint, and a child of God, whose spiritual and eternal welfare stands secure whatever befalls his crown. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the whole utterance as David placing his fate, in faith, into the hand of the Lord who is chastening him.

The Wesleyan Wesley presses the verse into a rule for every trial: that we may not complain of what is, let us see God's hand in all events; and that we may not be afraid of what shall be, let us see all events in God's hand. The witnesses do not divide; together they hold up David's here am I as the model of submission — not the numbness of despair but the rest of faith, a king who has weighed the worst and still commits himself to the wisdom and goodness of God. The older expositors mark the depth of it: David does not merely grant that God can do what he will, or has the right to, or will do it regardless, but cheerfully subscribes to whatever God will do, content that all is well that God does. It is the resignation of a penitent who has learned that the same hand which strikes is the hand he trusts.
27:
David sends Zadok back to Jerusalem with a word whose very form the witnesses debate — art not thou a seer? — and they agree that the priest is being enlisted as the king's eyes in the city. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out the crux: the Hebrew is difficult and must be rendered either as a question, art thou a seer?, or, with the slightest change of a vowel, as an address, thou seer. Either way Zadok is so called because he was now in some sort to fulfil a prophet's office — guiding David's course and making known to him the events unfolding in Jerusalem that would show God's will. Ellicott notes the curiosity that nothing is said in this narrative of Nathan and Gad, though both were still living, and that your two sons shows David means to address both priests, though only Zadok has been named.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke reads the dismissal as both reassurance and commission. As the priest of God and the one organ of the public worship, Zadok has no cause to fear for his safety, for the nation has not abandoned its God though it has abandoned its king; and David plainly wishes, through Zadok and Abiathar and their sons Ahimaaz and Jonathan, to receive frequent intelligence of the enemy's movements. John Wesley takes seer in its plainest sense — a seeing, discerning, observing man — which suits David's purpose exactly: Zadok is a wise man, fit to manage a business that requires prudence and secrecy.

The Reformed Gill agrees, glossing the title as prophet and as a knowing man who could penetrate into men and things, and so be of more service to David at Jerusalem than in the wilderness. The witnesses do not divide on the substance, only on the grammar. Whether David asks art thou not a seer? or names him thou seer, the effect is the same: the flight has need not of another sword but of a watchful, discerning head within the city, and David turns the high priest into his trusted intelligencer, weaving the priesthood, the ark's return, and the sons' errands into a single quiet strategy for his restoration.
28:
David appoints a place to wait for word from the priests, and the witnesses weigh both the location and the strategy it serves. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott raises the one textual question. The reading plain of the wilderness follows the Hebrew margin here and at 2 Sam. 17:16 and is supported by the ancient versions, denoting the wide valley of the Jordan about Jericho; but Ellicott judges the Hebrew text itself the better, reading the fords — both a more definite place where messengers could reliably find David, and a point of strategic importance where a retreat across the Jordan lay open at any moment.

The Reformed Gill locates the spot in the same direction — toward the way of the wilderness by which David and his people went after crossing the Kidron — and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative forward to its immediate result: Zadok and Abiathar accordingly took the ark of God back to Jerusalem. The verse is the pivot of David's plan: he will not press blindly on but halt at a fixed point, there to receive the intelligence his priestly watchmen will send.

The witnesses do not divide on the substance, and the small variation over the name of the place only sharpens the same picture. Whether David waits in the open plain or, as Ellicott prefers, at the Jordan fords, he chooses a station both findable and defensible — near enough to the city for news to reach him quickly, and poised at the river so that, if the worst come, he can pass over into safety. It is the mark of a man who submits to God's will without abandoning ordinary prudence, having just committed his cause to the LORD's favour yet still posting his messengers and choosing his ground. The commentators feel in this the same double temper that runs through the whole flight — faith that leaves the outcome to God, and wisdom that neglects no means God has put in reach — so that David's tarrying is at once an act of trust and a soldier's careful positioning while he awaits the turn of events.
29:
The priests obey, and the ark of God goes back to its place in Jerusalem. The source before the witnesses is brief, but the act is weighty, and they read it as the quiet completion of David's great renunciation in the preceding verses. The Reformed Gill supplies the practical detail: Zadok and Abiathar caused the ark to be carried back — that is, they ordered and oversaw its carriage by the Kohathite Levites, whose office it was to bear it, and themselves attended it — and there they remained, taking up the post David had assigned them within the city.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note simply that Zadok and Abiathar returned to the city with the ark of God, closing the episode of its brief journey after the king. The Puritan Matthew Henry, tracing the same movement, observes that the priests returned to the city to await the event — stationed there, with their sons ready as couriers, in the entire mutual confidence that had passed between the prince and the priesthood.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse's very plainness carries its meaning. What David asked in faith is now done in fact: the ark is not dragged through the wilderness as a talisman to secure a fugitive, but restored to the habitation prepared for it, left in Jerusalem under the care of faithful priests. The obedience of Zadok and Abiathar answers David's reverence — they honour both the sacred charge and the king's wish, and in returning become the very instruments by which he hopes to be brought back to see the ark again. The commentators feel the fitness of it: the throne is in exile, but the worship of God abides in its place, and the priests who keep it there keep also their loyalty to the LORD's anointed, waiting in the city as his eyes and his hope. It is a small verse of settled obedience between two storms — the flight behind and the mourning of the Mount of Olives just ahead. Henry's remark that they returned to await the event names the posture exactly: not idle waiting but watchful service, the priests holding their ground within the walls while their king holds his in the wilderness, each trusting the other and both trusting God for the issue.
30:
David climbs the Mount of Olives weeping, his head covered and his feet bare, and the witnesses read every token of his mourning and hear beneath it the sob of a wounded conscience. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify the signs: the covered head and bare feet are the recognized marks of grief and mourning (compare Est. 6:12 and Ezek. 24:17), and it is on this ascent, they note, that word reaches David of Ahithophel's defection. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke adds a darker shade to the covered head: it was the attitude not only of a mourner but of a condemned man, whose face was covered when sentence had been passed, as in the case of Haman (Est. 7:8). John Wesley reads the bare feet in the same key — a testimony of David's deep sorrow, humiliation, and shame for his sins.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gathers these threads into the chapter's most searching self-examination. Did it become a man of David's courage and greatness to weep like a child merely for fear of an enemy? Henry answers no — the tears were not for fear but for two deeper griefs. There was the unkindness of a son who came out of his own bowels now lifting up the heel against him; and there was, above all, the displeasure of his God, which infused the wormwood and gall into the whole affliction. His sin was ever before him (Ps. 51:3), never appearing so black as now; he never wept thus when Saul hunted him, for a wounded conscience makes troubles lie heavily (Ps. 38:4).

The Reformed Gill lifts the scene toward its greater fulfilment, noting that the Mount of Olives, named from its olive trees and lying a mile east of Jerusalem, was the very place where great David's Son, the antitype, so often resorted in his humiliation and from which he ascended to heaven. The witnesses do not divide; they see in the weeping company climbing the mountain a picture at once historical and prophetic. When David wept, all his people wept with him, and the older expositors draw the pastoral point — it is our duty to weep with those that weep, and above all to weep with those that weep for sin. Here the penitent king, mourning less the loss of his throne than the sin that lost it, treads in tears the mountain his sinless Son would one day climb in a sorrow greater than his own.
31:
News of Ahithophel's treachery reaches David, and he answers it with a single arrow of prayer — O LORD, I pray thee, turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness — and the witnesses read the moment as the meeting of David's deepest fear with his surest weapon. The Puritan Matthew Henry names the fear: nothing in the whole plot looked more threatening than that Ahithophel was in it, for one good head in such a design is worth a thousand good hands, and Absalom, no politician himself, had secured a man long acquainted with David's counsels. If Ahithophel can be baffled, Henry says, the conspiracy is as good as routed. David's prayer is short because he had no leisure for a long one, yet fervent — Lord, I pray thee, do this — and particular, naming the very man whose counsel he dreads, offered in firm belief that God holds all hearts and tongues in his hand and can take away the understanding of the aged and make the judges fools (Job 12:17; Isa. 3:2-3).

The Wesleyan Clarke agrees on the point of the petition: Ahithophel was a wise man, well versed in state affairs, and God alone could confound his devices — so to God David goes. The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the text and the setting. Keil and Delitzsch note that David received the intelligence as he climbed toward the place of worship on the summit of Olivet, where a high place stood; Charles Ellicott observes that a preposition has dropped from the Hebrew, which reads literally and David told, though the sense is plainly that one told David.

Henry draws the practical theology the verse teaches. We may pray in faith and with fervency that God will turn into foolishness the counsel taken against his people — David prays not against Ahithophel's person but against his counsel, that it might be given foolishly, or rejected as foolish, or, if followed, defeated. Yet, Henry insists, we must second our prayers with our endeavours, or else we tempt God; and so David will next set Hushai to countermine the counsellor. The Reformed Gill adds only that the report came from Hebron or Jerusalem. The witnesses converge: against the sharpest human wisdom the exiled king lifts a breath of prayer, confident that the God who governs every heart can make the wise counsellor's cunning collapse into folly.
32:
At the summit of Olivet, where David stops to worship, Hushai the Archite meets him with torn coat and earth on his head, and the witnesses read the encounter as the visible beginning of an answered prayer. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the rendering: better than where he worshipped God is where men worship, the Hebrew marking a customary act, for on the crest of the Mount of Olives, in all probability, stood one of the high places that abounded in Israel. Ellicott locates Hushai from Josh. 16:2, on the border between Ephraim and Benjamin, where he may have been at his own home when the rebellion broke out, and reads his arrival plainly as the beginning of the answer to David's prayer in the previous verse.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke fastens on the wonder that David, in peril of his life, halts on the mountain-top to pray. How true, he says, is the old adage that prayer and provender never hinder any man's journey — and he turns it on the reader with a question, dost thou do likewise? Hushai he names the particular friend of David, now greatly affected by his calamity, come in the tokens of deep mourning to share the king's grief.

The Puritan Matthew Henry knits the worship and the meeting into one providence. When David came to the top of the mount he worshipped God — weeping, Henry notes, must not hinder worship but quicken it — and it was then, some think, that he penned the third Psalm. And just then Providence brought Hushai to him: while he was yet speaking, God heard, and sent the very man who should become the instrument to befool Ahithophel. The Reformed Gill fixes the place simply at the top of the Mount of Olives. The witnesses do not divide; they marvel together at the timing. David has just prayed that the great counsellor's wisdom be turned to folly, and before he has well risen from his knees the answer walks up the hill toward him in the person of a friend. The commentators find in it a quiet encouragement — that God often has the remedy on its way before the prayer is fairly ended, and that worship offered in the depth of trouble is met, sometimes, with a mercy already climbing the mountain.
33:
David does not keep the newly-arrived Hushai at his side but tells him plainly, if thou passest on with me, then thou shalt be a burden unto me — and the witnesses read the words as the first half of a shrewd design forming in the king's mind. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke supplies the reasoning: Hushai was not a warrior but a wise, prudent, and discreet man, of little use in the field yet of the greatest use as a source of intelligence; therefore David directs him to form a close confederacy with the priests Zadok and Abiathar and to make their sons the couriers between Jerusalem and the king's place of retreat. To carry Hushai into the wilderness would be to waste him; to send him back is to plant him where he can do most.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the opening of the whole commission that runs to the chapter's end: David charges Hushai to return, counterfeit allegiance to Absalom, and thereby defeat the counsel of Ahithophel, reporting all he learns to the priests, who will send it on by their sons. What looks like a dismissal is in truth a deployment. The Reformed Gill marks only that David spoke after he had heard what tidings Hushai brought.

The witnesses do not divide; they trace together the second of the two weapons David had resolved to use against the conspiracy. Having first met Ahithophel's wisdom with prayer, the king now seconds the prayer with policy, refusing to let sentiment keep a useful friend at his elbow when that friend could serve him far better in the enemy's camp. There is a hard clarity in the word a burden: David loves Hushai, and Hushai has come in mourning to share his exile, yet the king will spend his friend where he is most needed rather than keep him for comfort. The commentators feel in it the discipline of a leader who, even weeping and barefoot on the mountain, can still think clearly enough to turn a loyal counsellor into the very instrument by which the great counsellor of the rebellion will be undone.
34:
David instructs Hushai to return to the city and profess himself Absalom's servant, that he may defeat Ahithophel's counsel — and here the witnesses confront the one moral difficulty of the chapter, for the king is plainly counselling deceit. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott states it without flinching: David here counsels fraud and treachery, and Hushai willingly accepts the part in order to thwart Ahithophel and weaken the rebellion; the narrative simply states the facts without justifying them. Yet Ellicott offers two considerations that temper the judgment. First, like frauds in time of war and rebellion have been practised in all ages and still continue; and second, David and Hushai had but slender knowledge of that revelation of truth and righteousness which enables us to condemn them, and so did with a clear conscience many things we now see to be wrong.

The Wesleyan Wesley is blunter and will grant no excuse: David's suggesting this crafty counsel, he says, must be reckoned among his sins — nevertheless God was pleased to direct this evil advice to a good end. The Puritan Matthew Henry stands with him, confessing frankly that he cannot see how this gross dissimulation which David put upon Hushai is to be justified even as a stratagem of war. The Reformed Gill notes only that Hushai was to return to Jerusalem, from which he had come.

So the witnesses divide, not over the facts but over the excuse. The great question is whether David's counsel of deceit can be defended at all: Ellicott mitigates it by the customs of war and the dim moral light of the age, while Wesley and Henry refuse all justification and call it plainly a fault. Yet even in their disagreement the commentators land on one confession that unites them — that God overruled the crooked counsel to a straight end. The stratagem was David's, and its questionable morality is his; but the defeat of Ahithophel that followed was God's, who is able to direct even his servant's sins to the accomplishment of his purpose without approving them. The honesty of the witnesses is itself instructive: they will not whitewash the anointed king, but neither will they doubt the providence that turned his error toward good.
35:
David completes the machinery of his counter-plot: Zadok and Abiathar the priests are already in the city, and whatever Hushai overhears from the king's house he is to tell them. The witnesses read the verse as the second link in a carefully forged chain of intelligence. The Reformed Gill gives its purpose exactly: the priests are there to assist in forming schemes directly opposite to Ahithophel's, and to receive whatever secrets Hushai can gather from Absalom's court. Hushai will be the ear inside the palace; the priests will be the safe hands to which his findings are passed.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the verse in its larger commission: David charges Hushai to communicate to the priests Zadok and Abiathar all that he should hear of the king's house, and then to send word on to the king through their sons. The Puritan Matthew Henry marks the same arrangement as an act of trust — David recommends Hushai to the two priests as persons proper to be consulted with, weaving counsellor and priesthood into one confederacy for his cause.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse rewards a look at the whole design now taking shape. David has met the danger of Ahithophel on three fronts at once: by prayer, that God would turn the counsel into folly; by policy, planting Hushai in the enemy's council to contradict it; and now by a line of communication, the priests receiving Hushai's reports and relaying them onward. Each part supports the others, and each rests on the loyalty of men whom Absalom's flattery could not buy. The commentators feel the quiet competence of it — a king stripped of his throne, weeping on a mountain, yet ordering his few faithful friends into a network of prayer, espionage, and swift report so exact that the usurper's own house will leak its secrets to the exiled sovereign. What Absalom won by stealing hearts, David will counter by the steadfast hearts that remain his, and the priests in the city become the hinge on which his hope of return will turn. Gill's plain word for the priests' task — schemes directly opposite to Ahithophel's — names the whole aim: to set a faithful wisdom in the city against the faithless wisdom Absalom has bought, and to let the truth, quietly carried, undo the counsel of the traitor.
36:
The chain of communication is completed by naming its runners — their two sons, Ahimaaz Zadok's son, and Jonathan Abiathar's son — by whom every scrap of intelligence is to be sent to David. The witnesses read the verse as the last careful link in the king's design. The Reformed Gill notes a practical precaution: the young men were not lodged within Jerusalem itself but stationed near it, with whom the priests kept a private communication, so that the couriers might come and go without drawing the suspicion that residence in the city would invite.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the arrangement to its close, joining this verse to the next: the two sons are the messengers by whom all that the priests learn is to reach the king, and the narrative's own grammar, they observe, marks the meeting of Hushai's entrance and Absalom's arrival as happening together. The Wesleyan Wesley confirms Gill's point about the young men's station — there means not in Jerusalem but in a place near it, to which the priests could easily send upon occasion.

The witnesses do not divide, and the small verse repays notice as the finishing touch of a whole strategy. Every element is now in place: Hushai in the palace to overhear and to contradict, the priests in the city to receive, and their sons just outside to run the word across the wilderness to the waiting king. David has thought of the very danger of discovery, keeping the couriers at a prudent distance from the watchful eyes of the new court. The commentators admire the completeness of the design — nothing left to chance, each faithful man assigned his place — and yet they never let it eclipse the prayer that founded it, for all this human contrivance rests on the petition that God would turn Ahithophel's counsel into foolishness. Policy and prayer stand together: David posts his messengers and trusts his God, and the two sons carrying news between city and wilderness become the living threads by which an exiled king keeps hold of his kingdom. Gill's and Wesley's shared note — that the young men were kept near the city rather than in it — shows how thoroughly David reckoned with the peril of discovery, so that the very placing of his couriers was itself an act of the prudence he would not let his faith excuse him from exercising.
37:
The chapter ends on two men entering Jerusalem at the same moment — Hushai the king's friend coming in as Absalom the king's son comes in — and the witnesses read the coincidence as the quiet vindication of David's plan and a meditation on the whole day's reversal. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the timing from the grammar: the construction marks contemporaneous occurrence, so that Hushai went into the city just as Absalom arrived — the loyal spy in place at the very instant the usurper takes possession. The Reformed Gill reads Hushai's entrance as pure obedience: he came into the city by David's direction and persuasion, in order to serve him to the uttermost.

The Wesleyan Clarke uses the closing verse for a long backward look over the ruin of the day. It was probably because Absalom and his forces were already near the city that David hurried his departure; and Clarke bids the reader behold in David a sad vicissitude of human affairs — the greatest of kings, a profound politician, a brave soldier, a sublime poet, a prophet of God and the deliverer of his country, driven from his dominions by his own son, abandoned by his fickle people, and for a time even by his God. See here, he says, that there is none so exalted that God cannot abase, and none so abased that God cannot exalt; God returned, and David's enemies were confounded. His crime was great, and God, who is just, saw fit to show his displeasure even at a sin his mercy had forgiven — for in all cases it is a fearful and bitter thing to sin against the LORD.

The witnesses do not divide, and the older expositors let the chapter close on its truest note. All David's wisdom and faithfulness, the Puritan Matthew Henry reminds us, are still those of a man in whom something needs forgiveness — but when the Son of David was treated with every treachery and cruelty, his wisdom, meekness, candour, and patience were perfect. How soon do royal palaces and cities change their masters; yet we look for a kingdom that cannot be thus shaken, in the possession of which we cannot be disturbed. The two entrances that meet in Jerusalem — the friend sent by the true king and the son who has usurped him — leave the reader between judgment and hope, watching a throne overturned by sin and a providence already at work to restore it, and pointing beyond David to the greater King whom the witnesses bid us follow, cleave to, and serve, in life and in death.
